planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring in moist, shaded sites; palm sedge can also be started from seed indoors with cold moist stratification but divisions establish far more reliably.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; palm sedge is one of the most dramatic native sedges, producing whorls of long, arching, bright green leaves arranged around the stem nodes in a pattern strikingly reminiscent of a miniature palm tree — hence the common name. It is one of the finest large sedges for moist shade gardens, growing vigorously under tall trees where few grasses survive. The golden fall color and persistent dried winter form add year-round interest. It spreads slowly by rhizomes to form loose colonies and is excellent massed under deciduous trees or along the shaded margins of rain gardens and ponds.
sources: Darke, Rick. The Encyclopedia of Grasses for Livable Landscapes, 2007

planting.note: Plant plugs or divisions in spring or early fall; space 6–12 inches apart and expect plants to fill in within 2–3 seasons. Pennsylvania sedge establishes slowly but once settled requires virtually no maintenance.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry soil in partial to full shade; Pennsylvania sedge is one of the best native lawn substitutes for shaded areas under oak trees and other large deciduous trees where grass fails. It forms a fine-textured, low, arching carpet of narrow dark-green blades that stays semi-evergreen through mild winters and requires no mowing when grown as a groundcover. It spreads steadily by rhizomes without becoming invasive and tolerates the dry, root-competitive shade under mature trees that challenges most groundcovers. Unlike lawn grasses, it requires no fertilizer, pesticides, or irrigation once established, and its dense mat effectively suppresses weeds.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in early spring in moist, shaded spots; fresh seed can be surface sown in fall for natural cold stratification. Tellima self-sows modestly and can establish small colonies over time.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; fringecups is a Pacific Northwest native woodland perennial in the saxifrage family closely related to heucheras and tiarellas. In spring it sends up slender wands of small, urn-shaped flowers with intricately fringed petals that open cream-white and age to rose-pink. The rounded, hairy, semi-evergreen leaves form a dense, attractive clump at the base. It blooms for an unusually long period of 6–8 weeks and performs well in the challenging dry shade of Douglas fir and redwood forests. A reliable, low-maintenance shade perennial that combines beautifully with ferns, native gingers, and bleeding heart.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring before new growth; fresh seed can be sown in fall for spring germination. Virginia waterleaf spreads by rhizomes and self-sows to form colonies in favorable conditions.

directions: Plant in moist, rich woodland soil in partial to full shade; Virginia waterleaf is a native perennial named for the distinctive pale, water-stained markings on its young leaves that look as though water has splashed on them — these marks fade as the leaves mature. It produces small clusters of white to pale lavender bell-shaped flowers beloved by native bees in spring, then large attractive foliage through summer. The young leaves and shoots are edible and were gathered as spring greens by Eastern Woodland peoples. It spreads reliably by rhizomes to create dense, weed-suppressing colonies under tall hardwood canopies — one of the most effective native groundcovers for deep shade in the eastern US.

planting.note: Plant container-grown divisions in early spring; fresh seed requires complex double dormancy (warm then cold stratification) and may take 2 years to germinate — divisions are strongly preferred. CAUTION: all parts of this plant, especially the berries, are toxic.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; red baneberry is a striking native woodland perennial grown for its spectacular clusters of brilliant red (or white in var. neglecta) berries on thick red stalks that ripen in late summer and persist into fall. The bold, compound leaves and fluffy white flower clusters in spring create an attractive layered presence in the shade garden. IMPORTANT: berries and all other plant parts are poisonous to humans and pets if ingested — plant in areas where children will not encounter them unsupervised. Birds eat the berries without harm. This is a slow-growing plant that benefits from being left undisturbed once established.
